skill.name: minimax-xlsx
skill.description: Open, create, read, analyze, edit, or validate Excel/spreadsheet files (.xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, .tsv). Use when the user asks to create, build, modify, analyze, read, validate, or format any Excel spreadsheet, financial model, pivot table, or tabular data file. Covers: creating new xlsx from scratch, reading and analyzing existing files, editing existing xlsx with zero format loss, formula recalculation and validation, and applying professional financial formatting standards. Triggers on 'spreadsheet', 'Excel', '.xlsx', '.csv', 'pivot table', 'financial model', 'formula', or any request to produce tabular data in Excel format.
skill.summary: Open, create, read, analyze, edit, or validate Excel/spreadsheet files (.xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, .tsv). U...
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: All 1465 static findings are false positives. The skill uses subprocess.run() to launch LibreOffice headless for formula recalculation (legitimate external command), OOXML namespace URIs are flagged as 'network' activity (these are XML strings, not network calls), and path-traversal patterns in xlsx_unpack.py are defensive zip-slip protection code that blocks malicious archives. The 'sudo apt-get' references appear only in informational error messages instructing users how to install LibreOffice. File I/O and zipfile operations are core to spreadsheet manipulation. No actual security risks identified.

medium_findings.title: subprocess.run() launches LibreOffice headless
medium_findings.description: scripts/libreoffice_recalc.py uses subprocess.run() to execute soffice with --headless --norestore flags for formula recalculation. This is expected and safe behavior for this skill's tier-2 recalculation workflow.
medium_findings.explanation: False positive for security concern. The subprocess launches LibreOffice Calc to recalculate spreadsheet formulas, which is the intended functionality. Uses tempfile.TemporaryDirectory for isolation and configurable timeout.

medium_findings.title: Zipfile unpack/pack operations
medium_findings.description: Multiple scripts use zipfile module to unpack and repack .xlsx archives. This is required for the XML-level editing workflow that preserves formatting.
medium_findings.explanation: Expected behavior. xlsx files are ZIP archives; unpacking is necessary for XML-level editing. The skill implements zip-slip protection in xlsx_unpack.py.

low_findings.title: OOXML namespace URIs flagged as network references
low_findings.description: URLs matching 'http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/' patterns in XML templates and reference files are OOXML namespace declarations, not network requests.
low_findings.explanation: False positive. These are standard OOXML namespace declarations required for valid xlsx files per ECMA-376 specification.

low_findings.title: File system read/write operations
low_findings.description: Scripts read and write files on the filesystem for xlsx manipulation.
low_findings.explanation: Expected behavior. The skill must read/write xlsx files to function.

content.value_statement: Create, read, edit, validate, and format professional Excel spreadsheets (.xlsx, .xlsm, CSV) with zero format loss, financial-grade styling, and formula validation -- all without openpyxl's destructive round-trip behavior.
actual_capabilities: Create new xlsx files from scratch using OOXML templates with proper structure
Read and analyze existing xlsx/xlsm/CSV files with detailed schema and data extraction
Edit existing spreadsheets at the XML level (unpack, modify XML, repack) preserving all formatting, styles, and formulas
Validate spreadsheet formulas, cross-sheet references, and named ranges for errors
Apply professional financial formatting including color-coded cells (blue=inputs, black=formulas, green=cross-sheet refs)
Recalculate formulas via LibreOffice headless when static validation detects issues
limitations: Formula recalculation via LibreOffice headless requires LibreOffice to be installed on the system; falls back to static validation if unavailable
XML-level editing requires understanding of OOXML structure; complex macros or VBA in .xlsm files are not modified, only the underlying XML data
Large spreadsheets with thousands of rows may require chunked processing through the unpack/edit/workflow rather than single-pass operations

use_cases.title: Financial Model Creation
use_cases.description: Build professional financial models with color-coded inputs, formulas, and cross-sheet references following banking and accounting standards.

use_cases.title: Spreadsheet Data Editing Without Format Loss
use_cases.description: Modify existing Excel files while preserving all formatting, charts, conditional formatting, and formulas that openpyxl would destroy on round-trip.

use_cases.title: Spreadsheet Validation and Audit
use_cases.description: Check spreadsheets for broken formulas, invalid cross-sheet references, and formatting compliance before sharing with stakeholders.
use_cases.target_user: Auditors, QA teams, and compliance officers

prompt_templates.title: Create Financial Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a financial model spreadsheet with revenue projections, expense breakdown, and profit calculations. Use blue cells for hard-coded inputs, black for formulas, and green for cross-sheet references. Include a summary sheet and detailed monthly breakdown.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a new financial projection model from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Edit Existing Spreadsheet
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the file 'quarterly_report.xlsx', add a new column for Q4 projections after the Q3 column, and update the total formulas to include the new column. Preserve all existing formatting and styles.
prompt_templates.scenario: Modifying an existing Excel file without losing formatting

prompt_templates.title: Validate Spreadsheet Formulas
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my spreadsheet 'budget.xlsx' for any broken formulas, invalid cross-sheet references, or formatting violations. Report all issues found.
prompt_templates.scenario: Auditing a spreadsheet before sharing with stakeholders

prompt_templates.title: Read and Analyze Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the file 'sales_data.csv', analyze the structure, identify key metrics, and summarize the data patterns. Tell me how many rows and columns, what the headers are, and any notable observations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Understanding the contents and structure of a spreadsheet file

output_examples.input: Create a budget spreadsheet with income, expenses, and savings tracking for 12 months
output_examples.output: Created 'budget.xlsx' with 3 sheets: 'Summary' (annual overview), 'Income' (monthly income entries with color-coded inputs), and 'Expenses' (categorized monthly expenses with SUM formulas and totals). All cells follow financial color standards: blue for inputs, black for formulas. File is 15KB with proper OOXML structure.

Validation report: 2 issues found. (1) Sheet 'Projections', cell G15 contains #REF! error -- references deleted sheet 'OldData'. (2) Sheet 'Summary', named range 'TotalRevenue' points to invalid range Sheet1!A1:A100 but data only extends to A47. All other formulas, cross-sheet references, and named ranges are valid.

best_practices: Always use the XML unpack-edit-pack workflow (never openpyxl round-trip) when editing existing spreadsheets to preserve all formatting, styles, and formulas
Apply financial color standards consistently: blue (RGB 000000FF) for hardcoded inputs, black (RGB 00000000) for computed formulas, green (RGB 00008000) for cross-sheet references
Run formula validation (formula_check.py) after any spreadsheet edits to catch broken references, error values, and shared formula integrity issues before delivering the file
anti_patterns: Never create a new Workbook() from openpyxl to edit existing files -- this destroys all existing formatting, styles, charts, and conditional formatting
Never skip the unzip step when editing xlsx files -- attempting to modify xlsx without unpacking corrupts the archive and loses data
Never ignore formula validation results -- unvalidated spreadsheets may contain silent errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, broken named ranges) that undermine financial analysis

faq.question: Why doesn't this skill use openpyxl to edit existing spreadsheets?
faq.answer: openpyxl destroys formatting when round-tripping: it loses styles, charts, conditional formatting, data validation rules, and other OOXML features. This skill uses an XML-level workflow (unpack xlsx, edit XML, repack) that preserves 100% of the original file's formatting and structure.

faq.question: How does formula recalculation work?
faq.answer: The skill uses a two-tier approach: Tier 1 performs static formula validation (checking for #REF!, #DIV/0!, broken references) using Python XML parsing. Tier 2 dynamically recalculates formulas by opening the file in LibreOffice Calc headless mode, which computes all formulas, then saves the result. Tier 2 requires LibreOffice to be installed.

faq.question: Can this skill read and modify .xlsm (macro-enabled) files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill can read, edit data, and validate formulas in .xlsm files. However, it does not create or modify VBA macros -- it works with the spreadsheet data, formulas, and formatting layers. The macro content is preserved during the unpack-edit-pack workflow.

faq.question: What financial formatting standards does this skill apply?
faq.answer: It uses a color-coding system: blue cells for hardcoded input values, black cells for computed formulas, and green cells for cross-sheet references. It also supports accounting-style borders, percentage formatting, year formats, and currency formats consistent with professional financial modeling standards.

faq.question: Is it safe to run LibreOffice headless on my spreadsheet?
faq.answer: Yes. LibreOffice headless mode opens the file, recalculates all formulas, and saves -- it does not execute macros or external connections by default. The skill uses --norestore and --headless flags for safety, and runs in an isolated temporary directory.

faq.question: What file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: Primary formats: .xlsx (standard Excel), .xlsm (macro-enabled Excel). It also supports .csv and .tsv for reading and tabular data operations. The skill can also produce TSV output when requested.
